options.explanation: This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.

This is incorrect. For the first blank ‘an’ will be used because the word after it ‘institution’ starts with a vowel (i). For the second blank ‘blind’ refers to a group of people (plural) so ‘the’ would be used and not ‘a’.

This option is correct. There is an institution for the blind in this city. "An" is used before the noun "institution" because it begins with a vowel sound ("i"). "The" is used before the adjective "blind" to specify which blind people are being referred to.

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.

This option is correct. The definite article "the" is used before "only English teacher" because it refers to a specific position or role that is unique within the context of this school. It specifies that among all the teachers at the school, there is only one English teacher, and that is the role the speaker holds.
This is incorrect as the article "The" is used.

questions.explanation: I work as the only English teacher at this school.The definite article "the" is used before "only English teacher" because it refers to a specific position or role that is unique within the context of this school. It specifies that among all the teachers at the school, there is only one English teacher, and that is the role the speaker holds.

questions.text: Complete the sentence:I think _ bread is stale.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: If the speaker is not referring to a specific loaf of bread, they should use the indefinite article "a". For example, "I think the bread in this store is stale."
An is used when ther is a vowel present, here in this sentence no the word bread doesn't start with any vowel thus using an would be incorrect.

The definite article "the" is used before singular or plural nouns when we are referring to something specific that is known to the speaker and listener or has been previously mentioned or is identifiable from the context. "The bread": This suggests that there is a specific loaf of bread that both the speaker and the listener are aware of or that has been mentioned earlier in the conversation. Therefore, "the" is appropriate here.
If the speaker is referring to bread in general, without specifying any particular loaf, they should not use any article. For example, "I think bread is stale if it's been sitting out for too long".

questions.explanation: The definite article "the" is used before singular or plural nouns when we are referring to something specific that is known to the speaker and listener or has been previously mentioned or is identifiable from the context."The bread": This suggests that there is a specific loaf of bread that both the speaker and the listener are aware of or that has been mentioned earlier in the conversation. Therefore, "the" is appropriate here.

questions.text: Complete the sentence: _ man is mortal.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The use of "a" indicates that the speaker is referring to one example of the noun, rather than a specific or previously mentioned instance of it (incorrect for this).
"An" is used when a noun or adjective that comes next begins with a vowel sound (incorrect for this)
"The" cannot be used as nothing is to be specified here.
No article is to be used here.

questions.explanation: In the statement "Man is mortal," the absence of an article ("a" or "the") before "man" is due to the fact that "man" is used in a general or universal sense. When we talk about general truths, principles, or characteristics that apply universally, we often omit articles. This usage emphasizes the universality of the statement rather than a specific instance.

questions.text: Demonstrate correct use of articles and prepositions:I bought _ umbrella to go out in the rain.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "A" is a term used before consonant letters hence cant be used before umbrella.

The indefinite article "an" is used before singular countable nouns that begin with a vowel sound. "An umbrella": This indicates any umbrella, not a specific one.Therefore, "an" is appropriate here because "umbrella" starts with a vowel sound ("u"), and the speaker is referring to any umbrella in general that they purchased for going out in the rain.
"The" is used when the identity of the noun is known hence it wont be reasonable to use it in this sentence.
This would be possible if we used umbrellas instead of umbrella but when talking about a singular thing an article needs to be used.

questions.explanation: The indefinite article "an" is used before singular countable nouns that begin with a vowel sound. "An umbrella": This indicates any umbrella, not a specific one.Therefore, "an" is appropriate here because "umbrella" starts with a vowel sound ("u"), and the speaker is referring to any umbrella in general that they purchased for going out in the rain.

questions.text: Demonstrate the correct use of articles and prepositions in the following sentence:Danial is playing tennis _ Sunday.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "In" is used to indicate location or position within or inside something: We went for a swim in the lake

“On” is a very particular preposition of time, that is only used with specific days, dates or named days. For instance, you would use “on” to refer to a calendar date, a day of the week, or a special day that can be named by its significance, such as a birthday or anniversary.

The preposition “at” can be used in multiple contexts, such as telling time or location (e.g., telling someone to meet “at midnight” or “at the coffee shop”). However, “at” can also be used to introduce prepositional phrases that identify the object of a sentence. He was looking at all the different options.

The preposition “of” can be used in many different contexts. It can be used to help quantify a time or measurement (e.g., “the fifth of September” or “three pounds of potatoes”) and even identify a location (e.g., “south of California”), but it can also create more general relationships between objects and their nouns.

questions.explanation: The correct preposition to use in this sentence is "on." So, the sentence would be: "Danial is playing tennis on Sunday." Here, "on" is used to specify the day of the week (Sunday) when Danial will be playing tennis. This is a standard preposition used to indicate specific days of the week.

options.explanation: The preposition "on" is used before the gerund "working" to indicate interest or enthusiasm about something. This construction is idiomatic in English when expressing interest in an activity or concept.

This construction is idiomatic in English when expressing interest in an activity or concept.

’The’ is a definite article that is used before a noun to indicate that the identity of the noun is known to the reader.

questions.explanation: The correct use of articles and prepositions in the sentence would be: "The butter is melting." "The" is used before "butter" because you are referring to a specific butter, likely one that has been previously mentioned or is known to both the speaker and the listener.

questions.text: Demonstrate the correct use of articles and prepositions:My brother's birthday is _ the 5th of November.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: 'At' is not used to refer to a specific day or date, rather it is used to specify a particular time.
We do not use 'in' here because it is not used to refer to a specific date, rather it is used in sentences where the longer duration of times are used. For example: In a month.
The preposition “on” can be used to refer to a specific time during which an event takes place.
'About' is used to indicate an approximation of time or a general idea.

questions.explanation: The preposition “on” can be used to refer to a specific time during which an event takes place.

questions.text: Choose the correct sentence from the following options
questions.subject: English

options.text: I thought it over very carefully before broaching the subject to Asma.
options.explanation: Correct sentence structure is present in option A, having proper use of subject-verb and object. Options B and C are wrong because of the use of "by", "through" and "over", which goes against the basic rules of subject-verb-object.

options.text: I thought it by very carefully before broaching the subject to Asma.
options.explanation: "I thought it by very carefully before broaching the subject to Asma" is incorrect because, "thought it by" is not a correct phrasal verb in English.

options.text: I thought through very carefully before broaching the subject to Asma.
options.explanation: "I thought through very carefully before broaching the subject to Asma" is grammatically correct, but it does not accurately convey the meaning intended in the sentence. "Think through" means to analyze or solve a problem by considering all its aspects, whereas "think over" means to consider carefully or thoroughly.

options.text: I thought over very carefully before broaching the subject to Asma.
options.explanation: "I thought over very carefully before broaching the subject to Asma" is almost identical to the correct sentence in option A), but it is missing the pronoun "it."

questions.explanation: Correct sentence structure is present in option A, having proper use of subject-verb and object. Options B and C are wrong because of the use of “by”, “through” and “over”, which goes against the basic rules of subject-verb-object.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the correct option:She returned after _ hour.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is incorrect as "hour" starts with a vowel sound so we use "an" instead of "a".

"An" is an indefinite article used before singular countable nouns that begin with a vowel sound. This option is correct as the indefinite article "an" is used here to specify that the duration of time before her return was one hour.

"The" is a definite article used before singular or plural nouns to indicate that the noun is a specific item or instance known to the reader or listener, or one that is being referred to specifically within the context. So, this option is incorrect as the indefinite article "an" is used here to specify that the duration of time before her return was one hour.
This option is incorrect.
